skill.name: biopython
skill.description: Biopython reference skill for computational molecular biology. It helps with sequence manipulation, FASTA and GenBank parsing, BLAST workflows, NCBI Entrez access, structural bioinformatics, and phylogenetic analysis.
skill.summary: Use Biopython patterns for sequence analysis, NCBI access, BLAST, protein structures, and phylogenetics.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a risk score of 100 because it treated markdown code fences, Biopython examples, placeholders, and scientific terms as dangerous code. Human review found no malicious intent or prompt injection, but the skill legitimately documents network calls, file I/O, API key configuration, and local BLAST command wrappers, so publication should carry a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate External Network Access Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Bio.Entrez and NCBIWWW.qblast calls that contact NCBI web services. This is expected for bioinformatics workflows, but generated code may send sequence queries or identifiers to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly call NCBI web APIs. The purpose is legitimate and documented, with no evidence of hidden endpoints or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Local BLAST Command Wrapper Examples
medium_findings.description: The BLAST reference demonstrates Biopython command-line wrappers that invoke installed BLAST+ binaries. This is a real external command capability when users run generated code, but the arguments are normal bioinformatics parameters rather than obfuscated commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly constructs command-line wrapper objects and executes them. The examples are transparent and aligned with local BLAST use.

low_findings.title: File Read and Write Examples
low_findings.description: The references show normal parsing and output patterns for FASTA, BLAST XML, JASPAR, and related scientific files. These examples can write local files when users run generated code, so users should choose paths deliberately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are conventional file I/O examples. They are not malicious, but they are real operations if copied into executable code.

low_findings.title: Placeholder API Key Guidance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged API key text, but the cited content uses placeholders for NCBI Entrez configuration. No real secret was found, and there is no code that collects or transmits arbitrary credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence contains placeholder strings such as an example email and API key. No actual credential material or exfiltration flow was identified.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command alerts appear to come from markdown code fence delimiters and command examples in documentation. The skill files are references, not scripts that execute backtick substitutions automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation fences or a prior static-evaluation explanation. They do not create an execution path by themselves.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Domain False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts align with scientific documentation terms and markdown references, not password hashing or encryption logic. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar cryptography used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The alerts occur inside bioinformatics documentation rather than authentication or cryptographic code. I did not find security-sensitive crypto usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance alerts correspond to scientific file parsing, database statistics, or structure inspection examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, privilege discovery, or environment harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static labels are weak signals in documentation examples. I found no semantic evidence of host reconnaissance intent.

content.value_statement: Bioinformatics tasks often need precise sequence parsing, database access, and reproducible analysis patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused Biopython guidance for molecular biology workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides DNA, RNA, and protein sequence manipulation with Bio.Seq and SeqRecord objects.
Shows how to parse, write, and convert FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, PDB, and alignment files.
Explains NCBI Entrez searches, record fetching, batching, and rate-limit practices.
Documents web BLAST, local BLAST wrappers, and BLAST result parsing patterns.
Covers protein structure parsing, residue inspection, and phylogenetic tree operations.
Provides module-specific references for alignments, databases, motifs, and advanced utilities.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install Biopython or external BLAST tools.
NCBI database access requires internet connectivity and a valid Entrez email setting.
Local BLAST examples require separate BLAST+ binaries and local databases.
Generated scientific results still need domain validation before publication or clinical use.

use_cases.title: Process Sequence Files
use_cases.description: Create scripts that parse FASTA or GenBank records, calculate statistics, and convert outputs for downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Search NCBI Records
use_cases.description: Build Entrez workflows that search PubMed or GenBank, fetch records, and respect NCBI usage limits.

use_cases.title: Learn Analysis Patterns
use_cases.description: Explore examples for alignments, BLAST results, PDB structures, motifs, and phylogenetic trees.

prompt_templates.title: Read a FASTA File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython skill to explain how to read a FASTA file, count records, and report sequence lengths.

prompt_templates.title: Convert GenBank to FASTA
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biopython guidance to design a GenBank to FASTA conversion workflow with clear file handling and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Search Entrez Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Database workflow with external network access.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Entrez reference to outline a PubMed or nucleotide search that sets email, handles errors, and respects rate limits.

prompt_templates.title: Build a BLAST Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython BLAST and SeqIO references to plan a batch BLAST workflow, parse top hits, and cache outputs safely.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks for a workflow to summarize GC content from many FASTA records.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan that reads records one at a time, calculates length and GC fraction, and writes a summary table.

output_examples.input: A lab member wants to fetch GenBank records for a list of accession numbers.
output_examples.output: An Entrez workflow that sets contact information, batches requests, handles HTTP errors, and saves retrieved records.

output_examples.input: A student needs to compare related sequences with BLAST and inspect the best matches.
output_examples.output: A BLAST workflow description covering query choice, result parsing, hit filtering, and when to use local BLAST.

best_practices: Set Entrez email and use an API key only when needed for higher NCBI rate limits.
Use streaming parsers and context managers for large biological files.
Cache downloaded records and BLAST results to reduce repeated network requests.
anti_patterns: Do not send private or unpublished sequences to public NCBI services without approval.
Do not load very large sequence files fully into memory when an iterator will work.
Do not treat BLAST scores, alignments, or tree outputs as final conclusions without biological review.

faq.question: Does this skill install Biopython?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference guidance. Users must install Biopython and required external tools separately.

faq.question: Can it access NCBI databases?
faq.answer: It documents Bio.Entrez and BLAST web workflows. Running those workflows requires internet access and NCBI-compliant settings.

faq.question: Does it include real API keys?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses placeholders for Entrez email and API key configuration.

faq.question: Can it handle local BLAST?
faq.answer: Yes, it explains Biopython command wrappers, but users need BLAST+ installed and local databases prepared.

faq.question: What file formats are covered?
faq.answer: The references cover FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, PDB, mmCIF, Newick, NEXUS, phyloXML, and common alignment formats.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning for documented network access, file operations, API key placeholders, and local command wrappers.
